melanocytic nevus: The image shows a symmetrical, well-demarcated papule with a uniform brown pigmentation.
This is a photo of a melanocytic nevus with regular, smooth borders and a consistent color pattern throughout.
The lesion displays a homogenous pattern of pigmentation without abrupt edges or significant asymmetry.
On dermoscopy, a network of uniform, light-to-dark brown pigment lines is visible across the lesion.
The photo shows a small, round-to-oval macule with sharp borders, consistent with a junctional nevus.
A key feature of this benign lesion is its stable appearance, showing no significant changes in size, shape, or color over time.
The lesion presents with a cobblestone or globular pattern, composed of uniform brown globules.
This image depicts a compound nevus, which is a raised, dome-shaped, and evenly pigmented papule.
The pigmentation is centrally located and fades evenly towards the periphery of the lesion.
There is an absence of atypical features such as streaks, irregular dots, or a blue-white veil.
melanoma: The image displays a lesion with marked asymmetry in both its shape and the distribution of its color.
A key feature of melanoma in this photo is the irregular, notched, or scalloped border.
The lesion exhibits significant color variegation, with shades of brown, black, red, and blue-white.
An atypical pigment network, characterized by thick, irregular lines and blotches, is visible on dermoscopy.
The photo shows a blue-white veil, which is an area of structureless, hazy pigmentation often associated with melanoma.
Irregular streaks or pseudopods are visible at the periphery of the lesion, suggesting radial growth.
The surface of this nodular lesion shows signs of ulceration, bleeding, or crusting.
This is an image of a lesion with eccentric, structureless areas of dark pigmentation, a high-risk feature.
The photo shows a 'regression' pattern, with scar-like white areas and pepper-like gray granules.
The lesion is larger than 6 millimeters in diameter and has shown recent changes, consistent with clinical suspicion for melanoma.
benign keratosis: The image shows a lesion with a distinct 'stuck-on' appearance, sharply demarcated from the surrounding skin.
This is a photo of a benign keratosis with a waxy, verrucous (wart-like), and greasy surface texture.
The lesion displays multiple milia-like cysts, which appear as small, well-defined white-to-yellowish globules.
Comedo-like openings, visible as dark, keratin-filled plugs, are a characteristic feature in this photo.
The photo depicts a lesion with cerebriform (brain-like) fissures and ridges on its surface.
A 'fat finger' pattern is visible on dermoscopy, showing thick, finger-like projections.
The borders of the lesion are sharply defined, creating a clear boundary with the normal skin.
This image shows a seborrheic keratosis, which often has a varied brown to black color but a uniform underlying structure.
The lesion has a dry, crumbly surface that may flake off easily.
A characteristic feature is the presence of horn pseudocysts, which are larger, round, keratin-filled structures.
basal cell carcinoma: The image shows a pearly or translucent papule with fine, arborizing telangiectasias visible on its surface.
This is a photo of a basal cell carcinoma with a rolled, raised border and a central area of ulceration or depression.
The lesion displays a classic 'rodent ulcer' appearance, with central erosion and a well-defined, pearly edge.
A key dermoscopic feature in this photo is the presence of shiny, white-to-red structureless areas.
The scan reveals blue-gray ovoid nests, a specific feature of pigmented basal cell carcinoma.
Large, blue-gray globules are scattered throughout the lesion in this image.
The photo shows superficial spreading basal cell carcinoma, appearing as a pink-to-red, scaly patch with a fine, threadlike border.
Leaf-like structures, which are brown-to-gray and shaped like leaves, are visible on dermoscopy.
The image displays spoke-wheel areas, characterized by radial lines meeting at a central axis.
There is evidence of surface erosion or crusting, which is a common finding in basal cell carcinoma.
vascular lesion: The image shows a bright red to deep purple papule or nodule, consistent with a vascular lesion.
This is a photo of a lesion composed of well-defined, red, purple, or blue-black lacunae (lagoons).
The lesion is a cherry angioma, appearing as a small, dome-shaped, erythematous papule.
A key clinical feature is that the lesion would blanch (whiten) with the application of pressure, known as diascopy.
The photo depicts a 'spider angioma,' which has a central red punctum and fine vessels radiating outwards.
This image shows a pyogenic granuloma, a rapidly growing, friable red papule that often bleeds.
The lesion appears as a soft, compressible papule, indicating it is filled with blood.
On dermoscopy, there is an absence of a pigment network or other melanocytic features.
This is a photo of a port-wine stain, a flat, pink-to-purple patch present from birth.
The image reveals a hemangioma, characterized by a lobulated, 'strawberry-like' surface.
actinic keratosis: The image shows a rough, scaly, erythematous patch on sun-damaged skin, characteristic of an actinic keratosis.
This is a photo of a lesion with a red base and an adherent, yellowish or white hyperkeratotic scale.
The lesion has a 'sandpaper-like' texture that is often more easily felt than visually prominent.
Dermoscopy reveals a 'strawberry pattern' with surrounding erythema and prominent, white-haloed follicular openings.
The photo shows a cutaneous horn, a cone-shaped keratinous growth, arising from an underlying actinic keratosis.
This lesion is located in a sun-exposed area, such as the face, scalp, or forearms.
The image displays a 'rosette' sign, seen as four white dots arranged in a clover-like pattern under polarized dermoscopy.
The surface scale is clearly visible, appearing as a white-to-yellow crust on a red background.
The lesion's borders are often indistinct, blending into the surrounding sun-damaged skin.
The photo depicts multiple, scattered, small, scaly macules, indicative of widespread actinic damage.
dermatofibroma: The image shows a firm, skin-colored to brown papule, often with a slightly darker periphery.
A key diagnostic feature is the 'dimple sign' or 'Fitzpatrick's sign,' where lateral pressure causes central depression.
This is a photo of a lesion that feels like a firm, button-like nodule fixed to the overlying skin.
Dermoscopy of this dermatofibroma reveals a delicate, peripheral pigment network with a central white, scar-like patch.
The photo displays a lesion commonly found on the lower extremities, appearing as a small, hard bump under the skin.
The central area of the lesion is often hypopigmented or white and structureless.
This image shows a small nodule with a smooth surface and well-defined but not sharply demarcated borders.
A faint, patch-like pigmentation is visible surrounding the central fibrotic area.
The lesion is typically asymptomatic but may be tender or itchy upon palpation.
There is an absence of ulceration, scaling, or vascular structures within the lesion.
